content.text: Why Naming and Tagging Standards Matter

Prometheus, Datadog, and similar systems store metrics as name + label set. A metric named claude_api_requests and one named claude_requests are separate time series — they cannot be aggregated or queried together. When different teams or services use different names for the same concept, dashboards break, alerts fire inconsistently, and on-call engineers waste time figuring out which metric to trust.
Naming and tagging standards create a shared vocabulary. Once established, any engineer can write a Prometheus query or a Grafana dashboard without consulting the team that owns the service.

attrs.alt: Metric name anatomy showing namespace, base name, type suffix, and cardinality-safe labels

content.text: Service or integration prefix — always claude_api for Claude calls
For durations and sizes, embed the unit
Never abbreviate in metric names. 'req' instead of 'requests' saves 4 characters and confuses every query writer for years. Clarity beats brevity for identifiers that persist forever.
Canonical Metric Catalog for Claude Integrations
The following metrics should be present in every service that calls the Claude API. They form the minimum instrumentation set required to write SLO alerts, capacity dashboards, and cost reports.
Total Claude API requests attempted, by model, status, and service
Total Claude API errors, by error_type (timeout, rate_limit, server_error, parse_error)
End-to-end request latency including retries, by model
Total input tokens sent to the Claude API, by model
Total output tokens received, by model
Total retry attempts, by retry_reason (timeout, rate_limit, server_error)
Current depth of the job queue waiting to call Claude
Number of in-flight Claude API calls at any moment
Times the fallback path was used instead of a Claude response

Labels add dimensions to a metric. Every label multiplies cardinality — if a metric has labels A, B, C with 5 values each, the storage backend holds up to 125 time series for that metric. High-cardinality labels are the most common cause of out-of-memory crashes in Prometheus and excessive cost in cloud metrics backends.
Never use the full model version string with patch numbers — it creates unbounded cardinality as new versions ship
success, timeout, rate_limit, server_error, parse_error, circuit_open
Enumerate all possible values in your metric registration
The service that made the call — not the instance ID or pod name
Never include canary — use a separate canary=true boolean label if needed
Labels That Must Never Be Used
Unbounded cardinality — one time series per user
Aggregate in application code; expose only user_cohort or tier if needed
Unique per request — destroys Prometheus entirely
Use traces (OTel spans) for per-request data
Ephemeral — creates time series churn on every deployment
Use OTel resource labels for pod identity; not metric labels
Use a bucket (short, medium, long) by token count instead
Unbounded — error messages vary per request
Use error_type with a fixed enumeration
Define all metrics at module import time, not inside request handlers. Metrics defined inside handlers produce duplicate registration errors and are harder to discover.

# metrics.py — central metric registry for claude-worker service
from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram, Gauge

# Requests and errors
claude_requests = Counter(
    "claude_api_requests_total",
    "Total Claude API requests",
    ["model", "status", "service"],
)
claude_errors = Counter(
    "claude_api_errors_total",
    "Claude API errors by type",
    ["error_type", "service"],
)

# Latency
claude_duration = Histogram(
    "claude_api_request_duration_seconds",
    "End-to-end Claude API request duration including retries",
    ["model"],
    buckets=[0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0, 30.0, 60.0],
)

# Token usage
claude_input_tokens = Counter(
    "claude_api_tokens_input_total",
    "Input tokens sent to Claude API",
    ["model"],
)
claude_output_tokens = Counter(
    "claude_api_tokens_output_total",
    "Output tokens received from Claude API",
    ["model"],
)

# Retries and fallbacks
claude_retries = Counter(
    "claude_api_retries_total",
    "Claude API retry attempts",
    ["retry_reason"],
)
claude_fallbacks = Counter(
    "claude_fallback_activations_total",
    "Times the fallback path was activated",
    [],
)

# Worker health
worker_queue_depth = Gauge(
    "claude_worker_queue_depth",
    "Current job queue depth waiting for Claude API",
)
worker_active_calls = Gauge(
    "claude_worker_active_calls",
    "In-flight Claude API calls",
)
circuit_breaker_state = Gauge(
    "claude_circuit_breaker_state",
    "Circuit breaker state: 0=closed, 1=half_open, 2=open",
)

# handler.py
import time
import anthropic
from metrics import (
    claude_requests, claude_errors, claude_duration,
    claude_input_tokens, claude_output_tokens, claude_retries,
    worker_active_calls,
)

SERVICE = "claude-worker"


async def call_claude_instrumented(
    client: anthropic.AsyncAnthropic,
    model: str,
    messages: list[dict],
    max_tokens: int = 1024,
) -> anthropic.types.Message:
    start = time.monotonic()
    worker_active_calls.inc()
    try:
        response = await client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            messages=messages,
            max_tokens=max_tokens,
        )
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
        claude_requests.labels(model=model, status="success", service=SERVICE).inc()
        claude_duration.labels(model=model).observe(elapsed)
        claude_input_tokens.labels(model=model).inc(response.usage.input_tokens)
        claude_output_tokens.labels(model=model).inc(response.usage.output_tokens)
        return response

    except anthropic.RateLimitError:
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
        claude_requests.labels(model=model, status="rate_limit", service=SERVICE).inc()
        claude_errors.labels(error_type="rate_limit", service=SERVICE).inc()
        claude_duration.labels(model=model).observe(elapsed)
        raise

    except anthropic.APITimeoutError:
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
        claude_requests.labels(model=model, status="timeout", service=SERVICE).inc()
        claude_errors.labels(error_type="timeout", service=SERVICE).inc()
        claude_duration.labels(model=model).observe(elapsed)
        raise

    except anthropic.InternalServerError:
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
        claude_requests.labels(model=model, status="server_error", service=SERVICE).inc()
        claude_errors.labels(error_type="server_error", service=SERVICE).inc()
        claude_duration.labels(model=model).observe(elapsed)
        raise

    finally:
        worker_active_calls.dec()

Histograms in Prometheus require pre-defined buckets. Incorrect bucket selection produces inaccurate quantile estimates. For Claude API calls, bucket boundaries should span the range from sub-second responses to 60-second timeouts.
Cache hits and very short responses
Maximum — requests beyond this are outliers
Add a bucket at your SLO p99 target. If your SLO is 2 s p99, having a 2.0-second bucket lets you compute the exact fraction of requests below the SLO without histogram interpolation error.
After deploying instrumented code, verify that metrics are registered correctly before writing alerts or dashboards.

# 1. Confirm the metric appears in the Prometheus scrape
curl -s http://localhost:9090/api/v1/label/__name__/values \
  | python3 -c "import sys,json; names=json.load(sys.stdin)['data']; print([n for n in names if n.startswith('claude')])"

# 2. Confirm labels have the expected cardinality
curl -s 'http://localhost:9090/api/v1/query?query=count+by+(model,status)(claude_api_requests_total)' \
  | python3 -c "import sys,json; r=json.load(sys.stdin)['data']['result']; [print(x['metric'],x['value'][1]) for x in r]"

# 3. Check histogram bucket count matches expectations (should not exceed ~20)
curl -s 'http://localhost:9090/api/v1/query?query=claude_api_request_duration_seconds_bucket' \
  | python3 -c "import sys,json; r=json.load(sys.stdin)['data']['result']; print(len(r), 'buckets')"

A common anti-pattern is re-registering metrics inside shared library calls or across multiple modules. Use a single module-level registry and import from it everywhere. Python's prometheus_client raises CollectorRegistry errors if you attempt to register the same metric name twice.

# BAD: metrics defined inside a function or multiple times across files
def process_request():
    counter = Counter("claude_api_requests_total", "...")  # crashes on second call
    counter.labels(model="claude-sonnet-5", status="success", service="svc").inc()

# GOOD: import from the central registry
from metrics import claude_requests
def process_request(model: str):
    # ... do work ...
    claude_requests.labels(model=model, status="success", service="claude-worker").inc()
Multi-Service Consistency via a Shared Library

When multiple services call Claude, consider publishing a shared instrumentation library as an internal package. The library exports the canonical metric objects; each service just imports and increments them. This guarantees all services emit the same metric names and label sets.

# shared_claude_metrics/metrics.py  (internal package, published to your private PyPI)
from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram, Gauge

__all__ = [
    "claude_requests", "claude_errors", "claude_duration",
    "claude_input_tokens", "claude_output_tokens",
    "claude_retries", "claude_fallbacks",
    "worker_queue_depth", "worker_active_calls", "circuit_breaker_state",
]

claude_requests = Counter(
    "claude_api_requests_total",
    "Total Claude API requests",
    ["model", "status", "service"],
)
claude_errors = Counter(
    "claude_api_errors_total",
    "Claude API errors by type",
    ["error_type", "service"],
)
claude_duration = Histogram(
    "claude_api_request_duration_seconds",
    "End-to-end Claude API request duration",
    ["model"],
    buckets=[0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0, 30.0, 60.0],
)
claude_input_tokens = Counter(
    "claude_api_tokens_input_total",
    "Input tokens sent to Claude API",
    ["model"],
)
claude_output_tokens = Counter(
    "claude_api_tokens_output_total",
    "Output tokens received from Claude API",
    ["model"],
)
Correlating Metrics with Traces and Logs

Metrics, traces, and logs are more useful together than in isolation. A metric tells you that error rate spiked at 02:14; a trace shows which request failed; a log line shows the exact error message. The correlation is made possible by consistent labelling across all three signals.
Filter by model in Grafana; drill into traces filtered by same model

The service label in metrics must match service.name in traces and the service field in logs. A mismatch means Grafana's exemplars (dots linking metric spikes to traces) will find no matching trace, breaking the drill-down workflow.

Some metrics are expensive to compute at query time — for example, a rate over a 30-day window. Prometheus recording rules pre-compute these and store the result as a new metric. Define recording rules for any metric used in SLO calculations or high-frequency dashboards.

# prometheus/recording-rules/claude-slos.yaml
groups:
  - name: claude_slo_recording_rules
    interval: 30s
    rules:
      # 5-minute error rate (used in alerts)
      - record: job:claude_api_error_rate:rate5m
        expr: |
          sum(rate(claude_api_errors_total[5m])) by (service)
          /
          sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[5m])) by (service)

      # p99 latency for the rolling 5-minute window (used in dashboards)
      - record: job:claude_api_p99_latency:rate5m
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.99,
            sum(rate(claude_api_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, model)
          )

      # Token cost rate (useful for cost dashboards)
      - record: job:claude_api_estimated_cost_per_minute:rate5m
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(claude_api_tokens_input_total{model="claude-opus-4-8"}[5m])) * 15
            + sum(rate(claude_api_tokens_output_total{model="claude-opus-4-8"}[5m])) * 75
            + sum(rate(claude_api_tokens_input_total{model="claude-sonnet-5"}[5m])) * 3
            + sum(rate(claude_api_tokens_output_total{model="claude-sonnet-5"}[5m])) * 15
            + sum(rate(claude_api_tokens_input_total{model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"}[5m])) * 0.8
            + sum(rate(claude_api_tokens_output_total{model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"}[5m])) * 4
          ) / 1_000_000  # convert from per-token price to USD per minute

The cost recording rule uses the per-million-token pricing from the Anthropic pricing page (as of the current knowledge cutoff). Update the multipliers when pricing changes — incorrect multipliers silently produce wrong cost estimates.

Metric naming and tagging standards are a one-time investment with permanent payoff. Inconsistent names produce permanently fragmented dashboards; consistent names make every future query and alert trivially writable.
Follow the namespace_subsystem_name_unit_suffix convention in snake_case lowercase. Never abbreviate.
Register the canonical ten metrics from the catalog as the minimum instrumentation floor for any Claude-integrated service.
Use only approved labels with bounded cardinality: model, status, service, environment. Never use user_id, request_id, or error_message as labels.
Define all metrics at module import time in a central registry file, not inside request handlers.
Set histogram buckets to span 0.1–60 s; always include a bucket at the SLO p99 target.
Validate metrics after deployment by querying label cardinality in Prometheus before writing dashboards.
